content.problemStatement:
A **union** in C stores different data types in the **same memory location**.

This means assigning a new member overwrites the previously stored value.

You must simulate a union `Test` containing:


int a
float b
char c
You will receive:


An integer
A float
A character
After assigning each value (in the above order), print the values of all members.

Since a union shares memory, only the **last assigned value** is valid; others become **garbage**.


Input Format
Input contains three lines of inputs:



First line contains an Integer `a`
Second line contains a Float `b`
Third line contains a Character `c`


Output Format
Print three lines:


`After assigning int: a = <value>, b = <value>, c = <value>
After assigning float: a = <value>, b = <value>, c = <value>
After assigning char: a = <value>, b = <value>, c = <value>
`
Use the word `garbage` for invalid values and for float values print it up to two decimal places.
constraints: c is a printable ASCII character
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case, three lines follow: the first line contains an integer a, the second line contains a floating‑point number b, and the third line contains a single printable character c.
content.outputFormat:
For each test case, print three lines exactly in the following order:
After assigning int: a = <value>, b = garbage, c = garbage
After assigning float: a = garbage, b = <value>, c = garbage (float printed with two decimal places)
After assigning char: a = garbage, b = garbage, c = <value>

sampleTestCases.output:
After assigning int: a = 10, b = garbage, c = garbage
After assigning float: a = garbage, b = 3.50, c = garbage
After assigning char: a = garbage, b = garbage, c = Z
sampleTestCases.explanation: The integer 10 is read first and printed with garbage for the other members. Then 3.5 is read and printed as the valid float. Finally character Z is read and printed as the valid char.

sampleTestCases.output:
After assigning int: a = 1000, b = garbage, c = garbage
After assigning float: a = garbage, b = 0.00, c = garbage
After assigning char: a = garbage, b = garbage, c = A
sampleTestCases.explanation: Demonstrates handling of the maximum allowed integer and a zero float.

sampleTestCases.output:
After assigning int: a = 1, b = garbage, c = garbage
After assigning float: a = garbage, b = 1.00, c = garbage
After assigning char: a = garbage, b = garbage, c = 1
sampleTestCases.explanation: All three values are distinct but look similar; the program treats them according to their type.

hidden.label: minimum boundary
hidden.expectedOutput:
After assigning int: a = 1, b = garbage, c = garbage
After assigning float: a = garbage, b = 0.00, c = garbage
After assigning char: a = garbage, b = garbage, c = a

hidden.label: maximum boundary
hidden.expectedOutput:
After assigning int: a = 1000, b = garbage, c = garbage
After assigning float: a = garbage, b = 1000.00, c = garbage
After assigning char: a = garbage, b = garbage, c = ~

hidden.label: uniform values
hidden.expectedOutput:
After assigning int: a = 5, b = garbage, c = garbage
After assigning float: a = garbage, b = 5.00, c = garbage
After assigning char: a = garbage, b = garbage, c = 5

hidden.label: multiple test cases
hidden.expectedOutput:
After assigning int: a = 10, b = garbage, c = garbage
After assigning float: a = garbage, b = 2.50, c = garbage
After assigning char: a = garbage, b = garbage, c = Y
After assigning int: a = 999, b = garbage, c = garbage
After assigning float: a = garbage, b = 0.01, c = garbage
After assigning char: a = garbage, b = garbage, c = Z

hidden.label: stress/general
hidden.expectedOutput:
After assigning int: a = 1, b = garbage, c = garbage
After assigning float: a = garbage, b = 1.23, c = garbage
After assigning char: a = garbage, b = garbage, c = a
After assigning int: a = 100, b = garbage, c = garbage
After assigning float: a = garbage, b = 99.99, c = garbage
After assigning char: a = garbage, b = garbage, c = b
After assigning int: a = 500, b = garbage, c = garbage
After assigning float: a = garbage, b = 0.50, c = garbage
After assigning char: a = garbage, b = garbage, c = c

solution.approach: The problem only asks to simulate the behaviour of a C union where only the last assigned member holds a valid value. For each test case we read the three values in the given order and immediately output the three required lines, substituting the actual value for the member that was just assigned and the word "garbage" for the other two members. No data needs to be stored between the three prints, so the algorithm runs in O(1) time per test case.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T) – constant work for each test case.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) – only a few primitive variables are used.
